findings.summary: Social-media claim that The Lallantop deliberately omitted naming accused in a UP minor-rape report to hide that they were Muslim; the fact-check examines whether the outlet concealed the accused's religion.
findings.attribution_reasoning: The allegation is a social-media claim that The Lallantop concealed the accused's religion (a claim about the outlet); Alt News treats it as a false/misattributed social-media post and there is no evidence thelallantop.com itself published the claim.

findings.summary: Investigation identified 24jours.com as one of several sites regularly flagged as spreading disinformation (e.g., health disinformation and copy-pasting content from Sputnik/RT) and as part of an Africa-based network that built fake media outlets and clickbait sites for profit.
findings.attribution_reasoning: The referenced investigation and article identifying sites (including 24jours.com) is hosted on disinfo.eu (the provided URL), indicating a third-party publication rather than content published by 24Jours.com.

findings.summary: Rush Limbaugh (RushLimbaugh.com) made a claim about George Washington's religious views in a post titled 'A Teacher on the Smallest Minority' (Apr 8, 2009).
findings.attribution_reasoning: PolitiFact cites a specific Apr 8, 2009 post titled 'A Teacher on the Smallest Minority' by Rush Limbaugh, indicating the statement originated in a RushLimbaugh.com publication.

findings.summary: A social media video was claimed to show 'geoengineering' happening as Hurricane Milton approached Florida; the video actually was uploaded in 2021 and does not show geoengineering related to Hurricane Milton.
findings.attribution_reasoning: PolitiFact identifies the claim as coming from Instagram posts (a social‑media user/video upload) and the World Meteorological Organization is not involved or cited, so the claim was published by a third party on instagram.com.

findings.summary: Quote attributed to White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt — 'Jesus didn't have electricity' — which circulated online; the claim gained traction after Irish Star published an article treating the quote as factual.
findings.attribution_reasoning: The Snopes fact-check states the quote gained traction after the Irish Star published an article treating the quote as factual, indicating the outlet itself published the claim.

findings.summary: Same claim: the 'Jesus didn't have electricity' quote falsely attributed to Karoline Leavitt; Irish Star reported the rumor as if true.
findings.attribution_reasoning: Snopes explicitly notes that Irish Star reported the rumor as if true, which indicates the claim was published by the outlet rather than merely being about or citing it.

findings.summary: Claim that George W. Bush was hanged at Guantanamo Bay (article republished on BestNewsHere.com).
findings.attribution_reasoning: Snopes documents that the false story about George W. Bush being hanged at Guantanamo was republished on BestNewsHere, indicating the outlet itself published the claim.

findings.summary: Claim (published by bestnewshere.com) that the U.S. Supreme Court ruled the COVID pathogen is not a vaccine / that COVID 'vaccine' is unsafe.
findings.attribution_reasoning: LeadStories identifies the false Supreme Court/COVID vaccine claim as coming from an article published on BestNewsHere, so the outlet itself published the claim.

findings.summary: Claim that researchers found a mummified, three-fingered 'alien' (viral story promoted on Gaia.com/Gaiam TV). Snopes examined the viral images and reporting and noted Gaia.com's history of promoting spurious pseudoscience.
findings.attribution_reasoning: Snopes specifically notes the viral 'three-fingered alien' story was promoted on Gaia.com/Gaiam TV, indicating the outlet itself published or promoted the claim; confidence is moderate because attribution comes via the fact-check rather than primary archival capture of the Gaia page.

findings.summary: Daily Kos founder Markos Moulitsas said (on TV) that Turkey is an Arab country; the statement was scrutinized and he later admitted it was a slip-up.
findings.attribution_reasoning: The claim describes a TV statement by Daily Kos founder Markos Moulitsas (reported and questioned by PolitiFact) rather than a claim published by Daily Kos itself, but the original broadcaster/site is not identified in the evidence provided.

findings.summary: A viral graphic purportedly from ABP Live claiming 'why do Hindu girls die for Muslim boys' — the graphic circulated as ABP Live content.
findings.attribution_reasoning: The dfrac fact-check states the graphic was fake and AI-generated and attributes it as a hoax purportedly from ABP Live, with no evidence ABP Live published it.

findings.summary: A viral 'ABP–CVoter' opinion-poll infographic for the Andhra Pradesh elections claimed to show projections from ABP Live and CVoter.
findings.attribution_reasoning: The Newschecker investigation found the ABP–CVoter poll infographic to be fake and falsely attributed to ABP Live/CVoter, with no indication the outlet published it.

findings.summary: Scam-Detector's review gives uncoverdc.com a very low trust score and states the site is not labeled as safe (flagging it as high-risk/scam-like).
findings.attribution_reasoning: The statement is a characterization of Scam-Detector's review found on the Scam-Detector validator page (scam-detector.com), i.e., a third-party evaluation rather than content published by UncoverDC.

findings.summary: An alternative health website published a post claiming COVID-19 vaccines cause serious side effects (including claims linking vaccines to autism).
findings.attribution_reasoning: PolitiFact names GreenMedInfo.com as the site that published the false article about COVID-19 vaccine side effects; there is no evidence the claim was published by Alternative Daily.

findings.summary: Claim that pedophilia was being taught as a 'sexual orientation' in (California) schools; FreedomProject distributed/republished the claim.
findings.attribution_reasoning: The Lead Stories fact-check explicitly identifies FreedomProject as having distributed/republished the false claim that pedophilia was being taught as a sexual orientation in California schools, indicating the outlet itself published it.

findings.summary: Post (screenshot from Prntly.com) said President Barack Obama 'waited six months to call swine flu an emergency after thousands died.'
findings.attribution_reasoning: The fact-check describes a circulating screenshot that purported to be from Prntly but the claim was a false attribution/hoax rather than an article actually published on prntly.com.

findings.summary: A statue of Christ in India was 'dripping holy water' (reported by TheWorldfromPRX); Snopes found the liquid was from blocked drainage/toilet plumbing, not a miracle.
findings.attribution_reasoning: Snopes’ sources list and link to a 2013 The World (theworld.org) story/interview about Sanal Edamaruku investigating the Mumbai statue, supporting that The World published coverage of the incident.

findings.summary: Cory Booker’s marathon Senate speech was described as a filibuster (per TheWorldfromPRX); Snopes found he did set a record for longest speech but it was not a filibuster.
findings.attribution_reasoning: Snopes provides no evidence that The World from PRX published the assertion that Booker's speech was a filibuster (the cited The World link is background on Thurmond), so the claim appears to be a false attribution to the outlet.
